name: Wagon Wheel — Section Drill
description: Drill each section of 'Wagon Wheel' with extended time to lock in the chord changes and strum pattern. The full progression is G D Em C | G D C C at 150 BPM.

steps.name: Intro
steps.instructions: Strum the full 8-bar progression: G D Em C | G D C C. One measure per chord. This sets up the feel for the whole song — keep it relaxed and steady.
steps.notes: The intro uses the same progression as the rest of the song. Focus on getting the strum pattern locked in before adding vocals. Notice how the second half (G D C C) resolves differently than the first.

steps.name: Verse
steps.instructions: Play the verse progression G D Em C | G D C C. Sing or hum the melody while strumming to build coordination. The vocal rhythm sits on top of the steady strum.
steps.notes: Verse lyrics: 'Headed down south to the land of the pines / I'm thumbin' my way into North Caroline.' The C C at the end gives you a moment to breathe before the next cycle.

steps.name: Chorus
steps.instructions: Same progression G D Em C | G D C C but with more energy. Strum harder on the downbeats and let the chords ring. This is the big sing-along moment.
steps.notes: Chorus lyrics: 'So rock me mama like a wagon wheel / Rock me mama any way you feel.' Drive the energy up here — dig into the strums.

steps.name: Bridge / Solo
steps.instructions: The fiddle solo plays over the same G D Em C | G D C C progression. Keep strumming steady — you're the rhythm section here. If you know the melody, try humming it.
steps.notes: In a band context, this is where you lock into a tight groove and let the lead instrument shine. Keep your strumming consistent and rhythmically solid.

steps.name: Outro
steps.instructions: Bring it home with one last pass through G D Em C | G D C C. Gradually let the strumming soften, then end with a single strong downstrum on the final G.
steps.notes: Let the last G chord ring out. In a live setting, you'd hold the final chord and let the audience cheer.
